Just now

Claude Code loops — how i use themFor a while my Claude Code sessions had a boring pattern. I kept typing the same prompts over and over.
“Check if the build passed.” “Look at the open PR and tell me what changed.” “Summarise the new errors.”
All useful, but I was the one sitting there pressing enter every few minutes. I was the cron job, and that got old. Then I started using loops, and a good chunk of that babysitting went away.
This is a write up of what loops are, how I set them up, where they help me, and the places they quietly let me down, so you do not have to learn those the slow way like I did.
First, what people mean by “loops”
“Loops” in Claude Code is a slightly overloaded word, so let me clear that up before anything else. I have seen it used in three ways.
The first is the agentic loop, which is just how Claude Code works under the hood. It reads something, takes an action, looks at the result, and decides what to do next, over and over until the task is done. You do not switch this on. It is the engine.
The second is the `/loop` command. This is the one most people mean when they say “set up a loop.” It runs a prompt again for…
